It sought to align the municipality's efforts with the upcoming government decree on OSS and foster cooperation between the municipality and Alba County Council.

The technical assistance involved five key meetings with local and county representatives. Experts discussed the integration of energy poverty into the existing urbanism OSS or the creation of a dedicated OSS. These sessions focused on building coordination between the municipality and county, with training on best OSS practices and relevant policies. The intervention successfully increased Alba Iulia’s capacity to implement energy poverty measures. It fostered localgovernment collab ration and provided essential knowledge for future OSS setup, in alignment with the emerging legal framework. The May 2024 training solidified a roadmap for OSS implementation and coordination across local and county levels.
type_of_funding: EPAH Technical Assistance Scheme

intervention_type: Data collection; Financial mechanisms; Household energy efficiency and refurbishment
partners_involved: NOVA School of Science and Technology of NOVA University of Lisbon (FCT NOVA); Câmara Municipal de Cascais
professionals_involved: Member of a local/national authority; Researcher; Social worker
project_body:
The "Fundo Verde de Apoio às Famílias" (Green Fund to Support Families) is a financing programme launched by the municipality of Cascais (Portugal) to promote energy efficiency and home renovation projects. Beneficiaries can receive up to 10,000€ in incentives. Residents within the sixth tier of IRS (Portuguese income tax) are eligible to apply for funding or co-financing for improvements such as replacing inefficient windows or household appliances, installing photovoltaic panels for self-consumption, setting up solar thermal systems for water heating, applying thermal insulation, installing heat pumps, and more. The co-financing rates prioritize interventions focused on the building's passive components, with measures, such as insulation and window replacement receiving the highest support levels.

Vulnerable families in the first to fourth IRS tiers can access the Green Fund through a partnership with Private Social Solidarity Institutions (IPSS). This arrangement provides advance financing, which is higher and progressive for these groups: families in the first two tiers receive 100% co-financing, while those in the next two tiers receive 90%. Families in the fifth and sixth IRS tiers must apply directly to the municipality of Cascais by submitting the required documentation via the designated online platform. For these groups, financial support is provided as a reimbursement. To further assist residents, the municipality offers physical one-stop shops where residents can receive energy advice and support throughout the application process, helping to enhance energy literacy in the community.
project_title: Fundos Verde de Apoio às Familias

intervention_type: Data collection; Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Policy Support
partners_involved: Institute for European Energy and Climate Policy (IEECP); NOVA School of Science and Technology of NOVA University of Lisbon (FCT NOVA); Institute for Structural Research; UNIVERSITY OF PIRAEUS RESEARCH CENTER; GMINA MIEJSKA RUMIA; DIMOS PEIRAIA; Torres Vedras Municipality; AUSTRIAN ENERGY AGENCY; NADACIA HABITAT FOR HUMANITY INTERNATIONAL; EURACTIV MEDIA NETWORK B.V.
professionals_involved: Engineer; Member of a local/national authority; Researcher; Technician
project_body:
LOCATEE's overarching goal is to support local municipalities in addressing energy poverty through the renovation of private multiapartment buildings for vulnerable residents. The project will address the poor connection between the private housing owners and local climate and energy policies in Central, Eastern and Southern Europe. LOCATEE will achieve this goal by providing a toolkit for identifying energy-vulnerable households, matching tailored interventions to their needs, and integrating energy poverty alleviation activities into long-term strategies of municipalities such as SECAPs.

Specifically, the project will use administrative data to develop typologies of households and buildings, which will contribute to finding priority intervention locations. This process will be in line with assisting authorities and social partners in addressing energy poverty locally through institutional, cross-sectoral coordination solutions, including the establishment of contact points and appointed focus groups with housing entities. These mechanisms will facilitate regular knowledge exchange on renovation programs and targeted solutions for people experiencing energy poverty. Using a pilot evidence-based approach, the project will address financial, regulatory, social and technical constraints in renovating private multi-apartment buildings while developing the capacity of local authorities to become intermediaries of the energy transition process. As a result, relevant stakeholders will collaboratively design and implement comprehensive interventions and support initiatives explicitly tailored to benefit vulnerable households. This evidence-based and collaborative approach will be implemented in three pilot municipalities in Central, Southern and Southeastern Europe: Rumia (Poland), Torres Vedras (Portugal) and Piraeus (Greece), while ensuring scaling up the LOCATEE framework to 32 more municipalities and then to further regions and municipalities in Europe.
project_title: LOCATEE - LOCal Authorities to Tackle Energy povErty in private multi-apartment buildings
website: Relevant publications:; Analysis of Indicators and Datasets for Energy Poverty Assessment: The Case of Private Multifamily Buildings | LOCATEE

intervention_type: Capacity building and training; Policy Support; Stakeholders' Engagement
partners_involved: Aisfor; Ecoserveis; Institute for European Energy and Climate Policy (IEECP); NOVA School of Science and Technology of NOVA University of Lisbon (FCT NOVA); KILOWATT; Comune Piano di Sorrento; COMUNE DI CASTELLAMMARE DI STABIA; ASSOCIACIO PER LA GESTIO DEL PROGRAMA LEADER RIPOLLES GES BISAURA; AREANATEJO; PROSVASI; DIMOS AVDIRON; MUNICIPALITY OF TOPEIROS; E3
professionals_involved: Member of a local/national authority; Researcher
project_body:
As recognized by recent EU frameworks, local and regional authorities have a key role in the implementation of energy and climate targets to achieve climate neutrality in the EU by 2050. However, local policymakers, especially in rural communities, often do not have adequate knowledge and technical capacity to undertake socially just energy planning processes. ENTRACK proposal stems from this urgent need, addressing knowledge and capacity gaps of local authorities and supporting them in co-designing social energy plans that respond to the real energy needs of regional and local stakeholders, including vulnerable citizens, and that are also in compliance with the European and national energy policy frameworks.

Through the employment of participatory approaches (i.e. multi-actor perspective analysis, empowerment evaluation, and participatory ethnography) and the delineation of a co-designing, bottom-up approach that starts from local actors and integrates energy and social policies in the regional and sub-regional frameworks, ENTRACK will support Mediterranean rural municipalities in co-designing feasible, ambitious, and just energy plans. Specifically, ENTRACK will build and pilot methodologies and capacity-building processes in 8 selected rural municipalities in 4 Mediterranean countries: 2 in Greece (Region of Eastern Macedonia & Thrace), 2 in Italy (Region of Campania), 2 in Portugal (Region of Alto Alentejo) and 2 in Spain (Region of Catalunya-Pirineus).

The final goal of the project is three folded: first, to support the codesign of energy plans in the 8 piloting municipalities; second, to deliver social energy co-designing action-based training to build capacity of policy actors; third, to deliver a ready-to-use scalable methodology on how to co-design policies with citizens in rural Mediterranean area.
project_title: ENTRACK - Empowering local and regional authorities to design clean Energy TRAnsition plans through Capacity and Knowledge
website: Relevant publications:; Taxonomy of Social Energy Plans, Strategies and Policies; Stakeholder Matrix and Pathways of Engagement; Report on survey results and documentation of the full survey database; Report on Perceptions, Needs, and Priorities of Citizens Concerning the Energy Transitions

intervention_type: Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment; Financial mechanisms; Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Renewable Energy Integration; Transparency and information sharing
partners_involved: Altroconsumo; DECO - Associação Portuguesa para a Defesa do Consumidor; Institute for European Energy and Climate Policy (IEECP); NOVA School of Science and Technology of NOVA University of Lisbon (FCT NOVA); OCU- Organización de consumidores y usuarios; GLOBAL NEW ENERGY FINANCE SL; INEGI - INSTITUTO DE CIENCIA E INOVACAO EM ENGENHARIA MECANICA E ENGENHARIA INDUSTRIAL; BUREAUVERITAS RINAVE, LDA.; De Groene Grachten BV; ASOCIACION NACIONAL DE EMPRESASDE REHABILITACION Y REFORMA
professionals_involved: Civil society employee; Engineer; Researcher; Technician
project_body: HORIS will further develop an existing platform called “Green Menu” that was piloted for Portugal by DGG and FCT NOVA during an EIT Climate –KIC funded project. It aims to become the digital go-to one-stop shop of integrated home renovation services to support homeowners’ decisions all along the overall building renovation customer journey, particularly focused on energy efficiency and renewable energy. The OSS will be developed adding new features about home typologies for 3 Southern Europe countries: Portugal, Spain and Italy. HORIS main objective is leveraging citizens’ trust in consumers organizations capability to select home renovation professionals with clear accountability and quality assurance, to support consumers via a simplified and digitalized process designed on users’ experience. HORIS aims at breaking down long-standing barriers by empowering homeowners all along the home renovation process, so they make the necessary decisions on investments. The main barrier to be tackled is the lack of trust in the actual energy savings, the choice of the professional/SME and the cumbersome of the financing process. HORIS will offer financial, legal and technical solutions in order to give an overview of the economic incentives for all the actors involved in the home renovation value chain and design the business model for each specific target of household. The project will seek a high degree of replicability and include a clear action plan to communicate experiences and results. HORIS will act on different fronts to foster a better use of EU and national public funds and mobilize a greater share of private funds engaging multiple small and medium-sized stakeholders, including local and regional public authorities and non-profit organisations.

intervention_type: Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Financial mechanisms
project_body:
The measure is a response to the need for affordable financial support for improvement of housing. The Brussels Green Loan provides access to low interest loans for home improvements such as installation of photovoltaic panels, heat pumps, solar water heaters or insulation, ventilation or efficient heating . The works need to be eligible for Energy Premiums (Primes Énergie). The measure aims to make houses more energy efficient and green and provides cheap loans for investments that only pay off over a longer period of time and are therefore not prioritised, especially not by landlords.

The investments are direly needed as inefficient housing is rent-wise usually only affordable by low-income tenants who then carry the burden of higher energy bills and/or lower living standards. Furthermore, the measure is of rather simple nature and can - especially in combination with other measures such as Energy Premiums (Primes Énergie) - be very efficient and unbureaucratic. Main beneficiaries: owners/landlords and tenants.
type_of_funding: Regional funds by Fonds Brussels (Housing Fund) and Citizens funds by Crédal (cooperative)

intervention_type: Regulation; Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment
project_body:
Municipalities and housing corporations are looking for ways to stimulate energy-saving behavior. Energiebox provides citizens with a free box with small energy saving measures, advice from an energy coach and an energy-saving report. The measure aimed to increase awareness and help people save on their energy bills. This is a relatively small measure with great results: people save on average 128 euro p/year and rate the box/advice with an 8.1. It started in one city and has spread across the Netherlands. Thousands of citizens have been reached so far.

Main beneficiaries: general population and tenants.
type_of_funding: Local funds by Municipalities, housing corporations, landlords, private house owners

intervention_type: Renewable Energy Integration
partners_involved: Municipality of Barcelona
project_body: The Energy Assessment Centers (PAE) are a service of the Barcelona City Council that provides the information, attention and intervention necessary for people to exercise their energy rights and for companies that do not have access to basic subministraments.
type_of_funding: Local funds by Barcelona City Council

intervention_type: Regulation; Financial mechanisms
project_body:
The Flemish region handde out interest-free loans for low-income home owners to increase energy efficiency in housing. A joint family income of no more than € 31.870 (€1660 per dependent) or other conditions such as entitlement to increased reimbursements from health insurance funds or protected customers qualify people for the regular energy loan. The regular energy loan was up to € 15.000 and was repaid over a period of 10 years.

The energy loan targetted people who inherited an energy inefficient house (or appartment) and goes up to € 60.000. In case the obligations of the energy loan are not met, the loan needs to be repaid with interest, whereas the market rate of the year the loan was given out applies. Eligibility criterium for the loan were a poor EPC label at time of inheritance (E or F in the case of houses, D, E or F in the case of apartments). Anyone who undertook to bring the home to label A within 5 years can borrow up to € 60,000 interest-free. For label B that is € 45,000 and for label C it is € 30,000. Anyone who undertook to bring an apartment to label A within 5 years could borrow up to € 45,000 interest-free. For label B that was € 30,000. The measure was promoted due to inherited houses often with poor energy scores due to age, vulnerable consumers in need and conditions for loans invest low risk for state and is extra incentive for loanees.
type_of_funding: Regional funds by the Flanders region

intervention_type: Regulation
project_body:
The Energy Efficiency of Multi-Family Residential Buildings National Programme has been oriented to the renovation of multi-family residential buildings with a main objective to secure better living conditions for the residents in the multi-family residential buildings, heat comfort and higher quality of living environment through implementation of energy efficiency measures .

The implementation of energy efficiency measures in multi-family residential buildings will contribute to:

- higher level of the energy efficiency of multi-family residential buildings and energy costs decrease.
- improving the exploitation features for extending the life cycle of the buildings.
- providing conditions of living environment in line with the sustainable development criteria.
- Expected results and effects: Heat costs reduction for the households.
- Improved residential infrastructure and change of the urban appearance.
- Cleaner environment – saved green gas emissions (CO2, etc.).
- Extending the life of the building that will have a higher price.
- Benefits of the national programme: Achieving energy consumption decrease of the households and realization of cost savings through energy efficiency measures’ implementation.

The decentralisation implementation model where municipalities carry out acceptance of applications, evaluation, approval, provide funding, monitoring of the implementation of the measures for energy efficiency of buildings.

A mayor of each municipality is responsible for carrying out the whole process on the renovation of residential buildings, located on their area and for the selection of contractors under the public procurement act for implementing the separate activities on buildings. Eligible are all multi-family buildings, designed before April, 26, 1999 three or more floored buildings with six or more separate sites with residential use.

Funding-eligible activities are:

- Activities on structural reconstruction /strengthening/, overhaul depending on damages that occurred during the exploitation of multi-family residential buildings, that have been prescribed as obligatory for the building in the technical audit.
- Renovation of common areas of multi-family residential buildings (roof, facade, staircase, etc.). Implementation of energy efficiency measures, prescribed as required for the building in the energy efficiency audit.

Concomitant construction and assembly works related to the implementation of energy efficiency measures and the relevant rehabilitation of common areas of the building as a result of the implemented measures with energy saving effect. The attendant construction and assembly works are related only with the restoration of the initial state, broken as a consequence of the renovation of the common areas and the change of joinery in the separate site. The most effective package of energy saving measures for the building is financed under the programme, where energy consumption class C in line with Ordinance 7 of 2004 (for energy efficiency, heat storage and energy saving in buildings) has been achieved. Territorial range activities under the programme have been implemented throughout the Republic of Bulgaria in the 265 municipalities.

The programme has been implemented through a financial resource totaling 1 billion leva that could be increased if additional funds are provided.

The programme is promoted by the poor condition of many multi-family dwelling buildings:

- The high energy consumption for heating in these buildings.
- The state of energy poverty of their tenants.
project_title: Energy Efficiency of Multi-Family Residential Buildings National Programme
type_of_funding: National funds

intervention_type: Regulation; Data collection
project_body:
The measure consists of financial support offered to vulnerable consumers . They can co-finance the electricity costs through benefit payment. The financial support can be used by beneficiaries of the guaranteed minimum fee or beneficiaries of personal disability allowance. The amount provided up to a maximum of HRK 200 (approximately 26 EUR) per month.

The measure is funded by all electrical energy consumers: for every kWh there is a "solidarity surcharge" of 0,004 EUR. These funds are used to finance "Benefit payment for vulnerable energy buyer" measure.
project_title: Benefit payment for vulnerable energy buyer - Naknada za ugroženog kupca energenata
type_of_funding: Electrical energy consumers

intervention_type: Regulation
project_body:
This measure provides households with financial assistance to improve the energy efficiency of their housing. The budget differs per region. Its total budget was € 897,75 million while for the Attica Prefecture the budget was € 160 million.

The measure is co-funded by the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) and national funding through the Regional Operational Programmes (ROPs). The individual who applies to the measure receives a grant ranging from 35% to 60% depending on their income to perform energy upgrade measures to their property. The maximum budget for each building/apartment is €50.000 for a single apartment € 80.000 in the case of a multiple residents building.
project_title: Energy efficiency in buildings in the Attica Prefecture
type_of_funding: National (4th programming period ESPA) and regional funds by PEP

intervention_type: Regulation; Data collection; Financial mechanisms
project_body:
This measure provides households with financial assistance to improve the energy efficiency of their housing. Its total budget during the first phase was 897,75 million euro while for the Attica prefecture, the budget was 160 million euro. The individual who applies to the measure receives a grant ranging from 35% to 60% depending on his/her income in order to perform energy upgrade measures to his/her property. The maximum budget for each building/apartment is:

- 50.000 Euros for a single apartment
- 80.000 Euros in the case of a multiple residents building.

During its second phase which started in 2020, the project's fund budget is €161,000,000 (total budget: €199,640,000).
project_title: Energy Efficiency at Household Buildings (Εξοικονόμηση κατ’ Οίκον 2020) Programme
type_of_funding: European (ERDF) and national funds by the Regional Operational Programmes (ROPs)

intervention_type: Renewable Energy Integration; Transparency and information sharing
partners_involved: Région Wallonne (Wallonian Government)
project_body: Energy desks are physical places where people can get personalised advice on energy-related topics. This includes energy efficiency/energy saving, regulations as well as programmes and schemes and how to apply for funding or grants. The measure is a good practice as physical availability makes information more accessible for older generations and experts can provide personalised advice and guidance through schemes and programmes. Main beneficiaries: owners/landlords, tenants, vulnerable and general population.
type_of_funding: Regional funds by Regional fund - Wallonian Government

intervention_type: Data collection; Regulation
project_body:
The measure targets citizens, companies, or other entities (sports centers, schools etc.) alike and benefits companies the general and vulnerable population. The intended target group is people with a modest income and collective infrastructures. Higher-income citizens can also benefit from the bonus if they use natural insulation materials or request multiple bonuses. For low income households, up to 70% of the eligible cost can be covered by the bonus.

The works that are reimbursable are:

- Energy audits.
- Roof, wall and floor insulation.
- Glazing.
type_of_funding: Regional funds by Regional funds - Gov. of Brussels Capital Region

intervention_type: Regulation; Renewable Energy Integration
project_body:
In July 2018, AMORCE responded to the call for a national program on Energy Saving Certificates (CEE) launched by the Ministry of Energy and Inclusive Transition by proposing the experiment "Territorial strategies for reducing energy poverty - Pacte -15%".

The aim of this experiment is to reduce energy poverty among households with modest resources through an experimental approach that reaches out to households living in thermal sieves to encourage them to carry out energy-saving work. This proactive approach aims to increase the number of energy renovation works and significantly reduce energy consumption. It is based on three pillars:

- Strengthen the identification of households by cross-referencing socio-technical data (income, energy consumption and annual bill, surface area of ​​the home, etc.) in order to create a database of precarious households living in energy sieves
- Systematize home visits to assess the energy renovation operations to be carried out, identify similar situations (geographically and/or technically)
- Organize order groups for the completion of standardized work defined in advance with business groups - proposal of work at a lower cost and/or included in a turnkey approach.

The -15% Pact is not intended to finance the completion of the work.
project_title: PACTE-15% - Territorial strategy to reduce energy poverty
type_of_funding: Private funds; National funds; Regional funds; Local funds by PIG : General programme of interest

intervention_type: Household energy efficiency and refurbishment
partners_involved: ADEME; AG2R La Mondiale; Fondation Macif; National Energy Poverty Observatory (ONPE); Abbé Pierre Foundation; Agence Nationale de l'Habitat (ANAH); Schneider Electric Foundation; Solinergy Endowment Fund; Ministry of The Ecological Transition France
project_body:
SLIME are one-stop shops detecting energy poverty, implementing energy audits but also social diagnostics for identified households and helping the householders by redirecting them towards existing sustainable and adapted solutions to climb out of energy poverty situations. The selected households receive a visit from Slime advisors to conduct a socio-technical audit. They evaluate the household’s needs according to their revenue, the building’s thermal envelope, home appliances, etc. They suggest building retrofits, a change in their energy contracts, link households to entities capable of helping them alleviate their energy poverty. The intervention methodology and budget allocation of the Slime programme is designed nationally by CLER (a non-profit association), and deployed locally by authorities in SLIME schemes (50 to this day).

The implementation of the SLIME methodology by a local authority is co-financed by the ESC scheme. The activities developed include: Designing the scheme by local authorities. Responding to CLER’s call for applications by the local authority. Validating the scheme by CLER, if the methodology presented corresponds to that of SLIME. Launching the scheme and actions. Annual validation by CLER of the local authority's expenditure, the number of visits made and the conformity of the actions. Payment to CLER of the corresponding sums by the funding partners at national level (“the obliged” in the ESCs scheme). Redistribution by CLER to the local authorities of the sums paid.

SLIME is a good example of action on the local level to identify vulnerable households, and to organise the ecosystem of local actors. They guarantee individual monitoring and tailored help on more than one subject to guide people out of energy poverty. Advisers look at the households’ needs and provide an extensive range of responses, from installing small saving devices like low-consumption light bulbs to help in the renovation process of inefficient buildings. Beyond the local point of view, Slime is part of a large, national network allowing collaboration and knowledge sharing between multiple authorities. The existence of the energy certificates funding scheme is also an upside for reducing the necessary budget for local authorities, which directly contributes to launching the scheme locally.

With an annual budget of about 110,000 euros per Slime community, the project has:

- Benefited 52,000 fuel-poor households.
- Saved an average of 170 euros in water and energy costs in annual household bills.
- Carried out the first action for 84% of the beneficiary households.
- Carried out retrofitting works for 46% of the households.

The deployment of Slime depends partly on the availability of relevant locally certified actors. A collective looking to create a Slime needs to ensure that it can count on skilled stakeholders in the area, otherwise, it will need to train a workforce to become advisers.
project_title: SLIME - Service Local d’Intervention pour la Maîtrise de l’Energie
type_of_funding: Private funding from energy providers (“the obliged” within the frame of the Energy Savings Certificates Scheme (ESCs) and controlled by the Ministry of Ecological Transition)

intervention_type: Data collection
project_body:
The report complements the programme “Audits and intervention in homes in a position of energy poverty” of the Barcelona Provincial Council, carried out during the year 2018 and part of 2019 in different municipalities in the province of Barcelona. The report displays the information obtained through the evaluation of interventions in 100 households belonging to the programme. Of these visits, 99 were made in person, while the last visit was made by telephone due to the health crisis caused by COVID-19.

The aim has been to assess the tariff situation and the current consumption of the users compared to the situation they had before the energy interventions, analysing whether changes had been made to their bills, which and whether they have been maintained or reversed. Concerning consumption, it has been assessed whether it is there have been declines in consumption, whether these have been maintained or increased, and how it has had an impact on the economic expenditure of families.

The user's perception of the program through a qualitative questionnaire, has also been evaluated, and this way to assess the actions and tasks performed by the technicians who carried out the interventions, and the impact this has had on families. 1 - Design and planning The selection of municipalities whose town councils have demonstrated special interest in the programme and where the availability of the social services technical team, so that must: validate selection of households, collaborate to obtain the invoices of the supplies, provide contact of them, and obtain permission to do so third visit and allow the visit. This selection will be done in close collaboration with the technical team of the Barcelona Provincial Council which has coordinated the programme.

The selection of households is based on the analysis of the data included in the ENERHUB (specific online computer application) program and a selection of the 100 homes where the visits will take place, based (between others) to the following criteria: The households have made the second visit. Those households have all the bills required. The number of visits must be proportional to number of audits performed for each batch.

- Lot 1: 18 homes: Pineda de Mar, Premià de Mar, Canovelles i Lliçà d’Amunt.
- Lot 2: 16 homes: Sallent, Manresa, Súria, Manlleu, Vic i Torelló.
- Lot 3: 17 homes: Terrassa, Sabadell i Sant Cugat del Vallès.
- Lot 4: 18 homes: Viladecans, Sant Andreu de la Barca i Sant Boi de Llobregat.
- Lot 5: 15 homes: Vilafranca del Penedès, Vilanova i la Geltrú, Igualada.
- Lot 6: 16 homes: Hospitalet de Llobregat i Santa Coloma de Gramenet.

The validation of homes chosen was done by the social services professionals. Following their selection and with the collaboration of municipal social services, the users need to provide invoice supplies. In the case of households that do not have piped natural gas, will be looked at if they have a different source of thermal energy and, if so, the invoice will be requested from the users. In case this invoice is not obtained, the analysis will be done with those of electricity. Afterwards, the scheduling of visits takes place. The company contacts the beneficiaries directly to arrange visits and informs to municipal social services professionals of these visits. The company then receives permission to visit and have access to the necessary information. It needs homes sign an authorization and to obtain it, the intervention of municipal social services. The purpose of each visit is twofold: Study of the invoices of the current supplies in relation to those presented at the time of the audit for each invoice: Analyse if changes have been made to that invoice recruitment, which and if they have been maintained or if they have been reverted. If there were downward power changes, assess whether they have been maintained.

Concerning consumption, assess whether there have been declines in consumption, have been maintained or have increased. In relation to costs, evaluate whether the cost is higher, equal or lower, both in absolute as relative terms (€ / kWh or € / liter). In cases where part of the procedures the audit goes close with “change in process” check if the change is going carry out and if not, identify the reasons. Collect the perception of users concerning the program of audits, based on a brief questionnaire. The budget for these assessments is 18.000 €. This measure could be considered a good practice as due to the information obtained from the evaluation we can improve the audit programme, and identify those aspects that should be modified to have a greater impact on users and their health: from reducing their bills, have more comfort at home and achieve a change in consumption habits and in the empowerment of the beneficiaries.

Actions such as leaving more time between audit visits, commenting on the report given to users, so that they understand what conclusions have been reached about home improvements will be put into practice in future editions as as a result of this assessment. The programme is funded by the Barcelona Provincial Council that is a local government institution promoting the progress and well-being of the citizens of the province of Barcelona –311 municipalities–, which represents 74.4% of the Catalan population. The work model revolves around the Local Government Network Plan, which is based on respect for local autonomy and networking between local governments and the provincial council to build a joint action programme, under the principles of efficiency and economy of public action and that is specified in the transfer of technical, economic and material resources.
project_title: Impact assessment of the programme audits and intervention in homes in a position of energy poverty
type_of_funding: Local funds by the Barcelona Provincial Council

intervention_type: Regulation
project_body:
This law is among the most effective in the EU in providing vulnerable people protection against energy poverty. It was the result of a People’s Legislative Initiative (Iniciativa Legislativa Popular – ILP) put forward by APE, PAH and the Economic, Social and Cultural Rights Observatory (Observatorio de los Derechos Económicos, Sociales y Culturales – ODESC).

This initiative was developed with the first-hand knowledge that these organisations and social movements had received from directly affected people. The draft law laid out the urgent needs as shown by people’s day-to-day experiences, with the help of legal experts who were also members of these organisations. The content and strategy were shared with the grassroots members of the platforms and social movements. While signatures were being collected, advocacy work was done with political parties and meetings were held with their legal teams to negotiate technical issues and legal terminology. As a result, for the first time ever in Spain, a law forbade the practice of cutting off the electricity of vulnerable families.

This was achieved through the introduction of the so-called precautionary principle: before cutting off supply, utility companies must first contact social services in order to verify whether or not the customer is energy vulnerable.
project_title: Law 24/2015 on the housing emergency and energy poverty
type_of_funding: Catalan Parliament

intervention_type: Data collection; Regulation
project_body:
This programme focuses on the incorporation of non-conventional renewable energies, energy efficiency and reduction of energy gaps. It strives for the development of a communal energy policy, adapted to the particular conditions of the territory. It is innovative at the national level as its main focus is the development of renewable energies and energy efficiency for the communal population, who do not have access to these systems.

Main beneficiaries: the vulnerable population.
type_of_funding: Local funds, citizens funds and private financing of Local Energy Strategy, part of the Energy Community Programme

intervention_type: Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment; Data collection
project_body:
Changeworks' Affordable Warmth Service worked across Midlothian to support Melville Housing Association tenants to be warmer in their homes and to afford their energy bills. They worked to raise awareness of fuel poverty issues, and promote energy saving top tips.

A team of advisors and volunteers provide tailored in-depth support to improve energy efficiency, reduce energy costs and are specialists in resolving billing and debt issues.
type_of_funding: Private funds by Melville Housing Association and local funds by Midlothian Council’s Poverty Grant

intervention_type: Regulation; Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment; Policy Support
project_body: The measure introduced that if a household's gas and electricity contract is terminated during the winter months (between October 1 and March 31) because consumers are unable to pay their bills, a default supplier (Sibelga) takes upon the supply for this household until the end of the season. The duration can be adapted upon decision of the region under exceptional conditions (e.g COVID-19 lockdown extended the period to May 31).
project_title: Disconnection interdiction during winter - Verbod op afsluiting in de winterperiode-Interdiction de coupure en période hivernale
type_of_funding: Regional funds by Brussels region

intervention_type: Financial mechanisms; Household energy efficiency and refurbishment
project_body:
The scheme funds energy efficiency improvements in both elderly and vulnerable people’s houses, making their homes more comfortable, healthier and more cost effective to run. It provides standard energy efficiency measures at no cost to the household, such as:

- Attic insulation.
- Draught proofing.
- Lagging jackets.
- Low energy light bulbs.
- Cavity wall insulation.
- Energy advice.

By September 2016, the scheme had already upgraded 125,000 energy-poor homes.

Due to its success, the Better Energy Homes scheme was launched in 2009 (through grants), to households that do not meet the necessary eligibility criteria for Better Energy Warmer Homes project.
type_of_funding: European and national funds by EU Structural Funds Programme, the Irish Government and the European Union together with SEAI, Ireland's national sustainable energy authority.

intervention_type: Financial mechanisms; Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment
project_body: The pre-paid offer by EnergieRevolte provides low-income households an easy-to-use way of controlling their electricity costs and thus avoid falling into debt. Via the app, households can keep an overview of their power consumption at any time and compare it over time. In contrast to prevalent billing practice (i.e., monthly fixed advance payments with final accounting at the end of the year), the app enables intrayear transparency of household electricity consumption and thus prevents low income households to accumulate debt. EnergieRevolte is a spin-off of the public utility of the City of Düren (Germany) offering a new service to low-income households and other interested customers. Main beneficiaries: vulnerable population.
type_of_funding: Local funds by local Utility of the City of Düren, Germany

intervention_type: Renewable Energy Integration; Stakeholders' Engagement; Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment
project_body: The measure is dedicated to reach isolated people affected by energy poverty or not reached by previous surveys. 8 citizens are recruited for each campaign (12 months) to go door-to-door and connect these isolated people to the relevant organisations. They give advice, inform them on energy poverty, encourage them to ask for social support. The objective to employ simple citizens to go directly to people's homes is to establish trust, talking peer-to-peer in a human approach (isolated people are not systematically equipped with a computer for example). The budget is about 45.000 € per campaign. So far, 90% of the 1118 households expressed trust and 202 contacts passed on to the OPAH operation (programmed operation of housing improvement) to begin construction work in the identified households. The next action will target the monitoring of renovations. The measure is a good practice because it reaches vulnerable people, out of the usual detection network. It gives a second source of revenue to the 8 "locaux moteurs" for the duration of the information campaign (16 to 18h of dedicated work perr month) and can be adapted to any territory.
type_of_funding: Private funds by the Fondation Macif (charity), Fondation de France (charity), EDF (energy supplier), AG2R la Mondiale (health insurance company), National funds by ANAH - Agence Nationale de l'Habitat (national housing agency)

intervention_type: Financial mechanisms
project_body: This measure provides households with financial assistance to cover heating oil costs during winter time. The total budget was €106 million.
type_of_funding: National funds

intervention_type: Capacity building and training; Transparency and information sharing
project_body: The measure established that, on the basis of intensive, personal advice from socially trained energy consultants, low-income households are to be enabled to consciously control their user behavior and to sustainably reduce their energy consumption. It is one of few energy savings measures for vulnerable households in Germany that does not only focus on electricity, but on heating as well. It therefore has a wider scope and offers continuous help.
project_title: Energy Savings Project Nuremberg - EnergieSparProjekt Nürnberg
type_of_funding: Regional funds by the city of Nuremberg and the local utility N-Ergie.

intervention_type: Data collection; Characterisation; Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment
project_body:
The energy scan is a quick assessment of the building, similar to an energy audit, but not quite as detailed. A person comes to the house that the tenant/owner requested the scan for, inspects the building, gives tips about energy consumption, heating and lighting and implements minor energy-saving measures valued up to € 25.

The energy scan is free of charge for special target groups (vulnerable population):

- 'Protected customers' (people entitled to social tariffs for gas and electricity).
- Customers with active budget meters for electricity or natural gas.
- Customers of energy loans.
- Tenants renting through social housing company or social rental office.
- Tenants paying less that € 578 (depending on location € 528) per month.
type_of_funding: Regional funds by the Flemish government

intervention_type: Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Financial mechanisms; Renewable Energy Integration
project_body:
There are funds in some departments in France to bring financial support to families in need for renovation work as an attempt to develop simple financial schemes for small yet essential reparations. In the example of Hérault, households in need are detected by social workers of the departmental agency of Pignan-Mèze.

These social advisors go and visit them to assess the households' situation, then present the corresponding files in a monthly technical comittee (composed by: the departmental agency, a member of the commercial partner Gefosat - in charge of the technical advices, a jurist in a tenants defense association, etc). Afterwards households receive a second visit destined to advising on energy.

Gefosat presents reports and recommendations to the technical committee and it is decided which measures to take (buying small equipment, estimation of the cost of renovation work, etc). Financial schemes are discussed with the tenants or owners. Funds are given directly to Gefosat, not the household. At the end of the construction, Gefosat inspects what has been done and a monthly follow-up is done in technical comittee. There might be three categories of help: funding of urgent reparations (water leakage, broken windows,etc). buying small equipment to the household's disposition. funding for renovation work for the landlord (30% maximum, limited to 1000€) or the tenant (3000€ maximum).
project_title: Social funds for energy management - Fonds sociaux d'aide aux travaux de maîtrise de l’énergie (FSATME)
type_of_funding: Local funds

intervention_type: Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Data collection; Financial mechanisms
project_body:
The state of Wallonia grants subsidies to low-income households (ceiling: social integration income +20%) for works to improve living conditions . This may be the replacement of frames or exterior doors, insulation work, the installation of a stove, the lining of a chimney, the installation of a boiler or a water heater, etc.

The list of works concerned is listed in the decree of the Walloon Government presented on this page.

If the applicant is a tenant, he must first obtain the agreement of his landlord, if the nature of the work requires it (mainly real estate work)

To obtain the subsidy, the applicant must contact the CPAS of his municipality. It is the social worker who will ensure that the documents necessary for the introduction of the file are collected and who will launch the procedure via the administration.

Amount of the premium As of June 13, 2022, the maximum amount of the subsidy is €2,000. The subsidy may, in certain cases, be doubled subject to certain conditions relating to the nature of the work.

A period of 5 years (minimum) must be respected between each MEBAR subsidy application. If a subsidy has been granted under MEBAR, the application submitted after 5 years must concern a different investment (see order below for details and conditions of the different deadlines to be respected).
project_title: MEBAR II - Investment aid - Energy for low-income households (Aide à l'investissement – Energie pour les ménages à revenu modeste)
type_of_funding: Regional funds by the Government of Walonia

intervention_type: Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Financial mechanisms
project_body:
The Municipality of Karposh was providing 100% subsidies for the renovation of façades and roofs of MFABs from 2009. In 2017 the subsidies decreased to 70%, while in 2021 the subsidies decreased to 50%. The programme is implemented in partnership with Habitat Macedonia. There is a public call, and homeowner associations apply for the subsidies. The municipality can provide 50% subsidy for the façade and 30% for the roof of MFABs, up to €7,000 and €2,500 respectively.

Funding for 2021: €65,000.
project_title: Subsidy for house renovation from the Municipality of Karposh
type_of_funding: Local funds by Municipality of Karposh

intervention_type: Transparency and information sharing
partners_involved: Centre Publicque d'Action Sociale (CPAS)
project_body:
The Réseau de Vigilance is an informal network to meet, discuss and exchange around various practices related to the right to energy and the right to water. It is mostly attended by social workers, both from public institutions and the non-profit sector, and to a lesser degree by researchers, employees of regulatory bodies, etc. Meetings are regularly organised once every two months and they mainly consist of exchanges about practices on the ground, strategies deployed by each organisation to address certain systemic issues, debriefings related to recent legislative or regulatory developments, etc.

The measure is a good example of:

- Local platforms for knowledge exchange easy to set up.
- Training and presentations that can be useful to enhance the knowledge of social workers, researchers and other participants.
- Feedback loop for policymakers & (local) administrators.
type_of_funding: Local funds by CPAS Brussels

intervention_type: Regulation; Renewable Energy Integration
partners_involved: Lille Metropole
project_body:
A guide intended for all professionals who are wondering about the devices available, at the national level, to help households with modest resources who are having difficulty paying their energy bills or achieving a correct level of thermal comfort in their homes. It is a practical tool that aims to explain fuel poverty, how the phenomenon manifests itself in households, as well as the various resources that can be mobilised to support them in solving the problem.

It presents the key players at national and local levels, as well as the main energy poverty reduction mechanisms that can be mobilised for households:

- Non-works arrangements (advice and information actions, financial aid for the payment of invoices and unpaid debts, mediation actions and dispute settlement).
- Support systems for carrying out work (household support approach: technical, legal, financial support).
- Financial mechanisms allowing the financial closure of works.

Each of these devices is detailed in an action sheet which provides a general presentation of the action, the target audiences, the eligibility criteria, the typical course of implementation, a toolbox and concrete feedback.
type_of_funding: Local funds by the Metropol Lille, the municipality, the region and the Ministry of energy transition

intervention_type: Renewable Energy Integration
project_body: Impartial and independent advice on how to: Reduce your energy bills. Make your home warmer. Plan your home improvements. Make your home greener. Recommendations are based on standards developed for the government as a way of understanding how houses and flats use energy, and how your home’s energy use could be improved. The website offers different specific recommendations on: Energy efficiency, through a calculator. Home energy grants, promoted by the government. Boiler and heating systems. The government commissioned Each Home Counts Review acknowledged the importance of consumers receiving trusted, impartial advice on energy efficiency. The review also made recommendations on how energy efficiency advice could be improved.
type_of_funding: National funds by department for Business, Energy, and Industrial Strategy

intervention_type: Regulation; Renewable Energy Integration
project_body:
The Single-family Affordable Solar Homes (SASH) programme was one of the California Solar Initiative’s (CSI) two low-income solar programmes.

GRID Alternatives (GRID), a non-profit solar contractor, was the statewide Program Administrator for the SASH programme. The SASH incentive was available to qualifying low-income homeowners in the Pacific Gas and Electric (PG&E), Southern California Edison (SCE), and San Diego Gas and Electric (SDG&E) Investor-Owned Utility (IOU) service territories. The SASH was uniquely designed to be a comprehensive low-income solar programme. In addition to providing incentives, SASH was structured to promote or provide energy efficiency, workforce development and green jobs training opportunities, and broad community engagement with low-income communities.

There was no other solar programme in California that had such a diverse range of benefits for low-income communities. It was truly a first-of-its-kind solar programme. The SASH incentive provided low-income families with free or low-cost solar photovoltaic (PV) systems that significantly reduce household energy expenses and allow families to direct those savings toward other basic needs. GRID Alternatives’ volunteer-based installation model proved to be a highly efficient model that makes solar affordable for low-income homeowners while also creating valuable job training opportunities.

In addition to being the primary installer for SASH, GRID provided education on and access to energy efficiency programs that further reduce a household’s energy consumption and expenses. In implementing the programme, GRID Alternatives provided opportunities for local volunteers to assist with installations, engage their communities, and to participate in CA energy programs. GRID trained over 44,030 volunteers and job trainees in California to help promote and install solar in low-income communities since the inception of the programme. GRID required its volunteers to participate in a solar orientation programme that educated these potential solar adopters about solar PV and energy efficiency. Highlights and milestones include a total of 9,264 PV systems installed and interconnected. In total these projects encumbered approximately $132.3 million in incentives and represented almost 30 MW of solar capacity. The SASH referred over 10,250 low-income homeowners to the utilities' Energy Savings Assistance (ESA) programmes.

Eligible applicants must:

- Receive electrical service from Pacific Gas & Electric (PG&E), Southern California Edison (SCE), or San Diego Gas & Electric (SDG&E),
- Own and live in their home, Have a household income that is 80% or below the area median income (AMI),
- Live in a home defined as “affordable housing” by California Public Utilities Code 2852.

The SASH offered one incentive level of $3 per watt. The funding of the project reached $108M with D.07-11-045 and the reauthorized SASH program with $54M in additional funding through D.15-01-027. The programme provided direct economic benefits to participating families and added value to the industry with green job training and broad consumer education. Each SASH project contained a workforce development component and provided opportunities for job trainees and volunteers to get hands-on experience installing solar systems.
type_of_funding: Regional funds by California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC)- California Solar Initiative

intervention_type: Renewable Energy Integration
project_body:
The ULISSE corporation felt there was a need for monitoring and supporting households in energy poverty and that it could be done by workers in integration.

SOLENI is directed by ULISSE Energie - a company targeting occupational integration - and works in cooperation with local entities (called "prescribers") like the departmental council, energy suppliers like EDF and social housing providers like SDH. Prescribers identify targeted households (most times energy inefficient houses and/or energy poor) then address them to the SOLENI intervention plan.

SOLENI does a sociotechnical audit focused on well-being and living habits during the first visit. During the second, they install small equipment and can carry out small improvements like window draughtproofing.

If it isn't enough, they guide householders towards other social support or actors. Since 2011 they helped 1100 households for a total 30% of water savings and 15% of electricity savings. There is a good relationship established between the population and the advisors from SOLENI because of their relatable social situation. Service is free for householders. Benefits for prescribers are under the form of money saved on legal conflict with housing providers, health interventions etc.
type_of_funding: Private funds by ACTIS and SDH (social housing providers), local funds by Isère department, Centre communal d'action sociale (CCAS) de Grenoble

intervention_type: Financial mechanisms
project_body:
The programme benefits energy suppliers and the general population and aims at the extensive energy upgrade of existing homes located in areas under the control of the Republic of Cyprus.

The intensity of the sponsorship amounts to 60% of the approved budget of each application and can cover costs related to thermal insulation of the house shell, replacement of frames, installation of shading systems as well as the installation and/or replacement of technical systems (such as e.g. solar, photovoltaic, air conditioners, storage batteries, control systems, etc.). For the homes of vulnerable consumers, the intensity of sponsorship increases to 80%.

The programme motivates the general population to upgrade the energy efficiency of their home and reduce electricity consumption and CO2.
type_of_funding: European funds by European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) of the EU. National funds by the Republic of Cyprus

intervention_type: Data collection
project_body:
The survey conducted by the city of Sztum targeted owners of and families living in single-family houses in a quarter limited by Chopin, Kopernika, Skłodowska and Konopnicka streets. There are approximately 50 houses in the quarter.

The survey aimed at:

collecting basic data about buildings in the designated area: number, type, technical condition, thermal insulation (if existing, if yes - what type), past investments made.

collecting basic demographic data on the residents (number of people in the household, their age, average income, etc.).

assessing the resident's state of knowledge about available solutions to improve energy efficiency of the building.

assessing the popularity of different existing solutions to improve energy efficiency of buildings.

The study was carried out as a part of a German-Polish cities cooperation focusing on the development of low-carbon economy. District selected for the survey is one of the oldest in the city, with old buildings significantly contributing to the local air pollution. The survey was the first stage of larger research and aimed at selecting buildings to be offered free thermal audit (to be conducted if owners express their interest. The questionnaire was prepared by the researcher in consultation with the Ordering Party - Adelphi Foundation (coordinator of the umbrella project) and the City of Sztum. The data obtained from the survey will be a basis for further measures aiming at the reduction of low-stack emissions in the city and thus - the health conditions and comfort of the citizens. It will also help to prepare the appropriate assistance programme for those affected by energy poverty.
project_title: Survey among single-family house owners in Sztum
type_of_funding: Regional and local funds by the Voivodeship fund for Environmental Protection and Water Management.

intervention_type: Regulation
project_body:
The Municipality of Budapest established a non-profit organisation (Network Foundation / Hálózat Alapítvány) which provides financial support to low-income consumers by subsidising their utility bills for one year , providing allowance for repaying arrears or extraordinary aid in case of crisis. The foundation was funded by the Municipality of Budapest, but could collect private donations as well. It was shut down in 2012, but has been available again since September 2020.

Each beneficiary receives 20.000 HUF (€56) per year.

Funding: ~€2.45 million in 2009 (856 million HUF).
project_title: Utility subsidy by the Municipality of Budapest (Fővárosi rezsitámogatás)
type_of_funding: Private funds (donations), local funds by the municipality of Budapest

intervention_type: Renewable Energy Integration
project_body:
The measure established that Local Energy Agencies visit households to observe the building, equipment, the household's habits , etc. After the visit, the operator gives advices to the occupants, can install small equipment to save water and energy, help obtain financial aids for further renovations . Households are spotted by a network associated with FSL (fonds social logement). Households targeted are those where energy and water bills are high or where the indoor temperature is below the recommendations in winter. The programme is now (since 2018) registered in the SLIME network as a SDIME.

Budget : in 2018, €17500 and in 2019, €200 000 used to finance the visits (included the operators) and one responsible.

Key Performance Indicators: 600 households followed since 2007.
project_title: Visites eau énergie (VEE) / Water and energy visits
type_of_funding: National funds by CLER, local funds by the departmental council

intervention_type: Renewable Energy Integration
project_body:
Vermont has one of the most aggressive suites of clean energy goals (EAN, 2020) in the United States. The state has set expectations high: while we have missed the goal of weatherizing 80,000 existing homes by 2020, we continue to strive towards other goals such as zero-energy new construction codes by 2030 and 90% renewable energy for all sectors by 2050 (Vermont DPS, 2016). To accomplish this, Vermont needs a thriving, eager and savvy home performance workforce to deliver results in the thermal sector.

The Building Performance Professionals Association of Vermont (BPPA-VT) offers Zero Energy Now in 2020! Homeowners can participate in a comprehensive energy retrofit program providing weatherization, efficient heating solutions, and solar PV to your home, drastically reducing energy costs and greenhouse gas emissions. Avoid the burden of researching and hiring separate contractors to perform efficiency upgrades to your household by working with a Zero Energy Now-certified energy contractor (CEC).

The CEC will work with you to design and develop a custom project work scope that applies the optimal combination of weatherization, high-efficiency mechanicals, and renewable energy to achieve the greatest savings at the best potential cost/benefit to you. Through Zero Energy Now, consumers also learn about current cash incentives and information on special low-cost financing options. In 2015, BPPA-VT secured funding from the state’s largest utility, Green Mountain Power (GMP), to pilot “Zero Energy Now,” a comprehensive, integrated “deep savings” approach that was implemented in the following year. For each participant, the minimum programme standards required a 10% reduction in the use of energy (e.g., through weatherization), a 50% reduction in the use of fossil fuels (achieved predominantly through weatherization and heat pumps) and grid electricity, and that 50% of household energy consumption is met by the use of renewables on-site (also referred to as “10-50-50”).

CECs will: Complete a detailed energy analysis of your home or business offer options that combine:

- efficiency upgrades (including air sealing, insulation and heat pumps)
- renewable heating sources (such as replacing an oil furnace with a wood/pellet system)
- solar power. Manage your project from start to finish. Help you get available rebates and cash incentives Work with lenders across the state to find special low-cost financing for your energy saving project
type_of_funding: Private funds by Green Mountain Power (GMP)

intervention_type: Regulation; Renewable Energy Integration
project_body: The Sponsorship Plan aims to provide financial incentives , in the form of government sponsorship, to encourage the use of RES and implement EXE measures in existing buildings that are legally used as residences and to contribute to the achievement of national RES and EXE obligations. The Plan has been included in the proposals for possible funding from the Recovery and Sustainability Mechanism (Regulation (EU) 2021/241).For mountainous areas the sponsorship amount is doubled (100% increase).
project_title: Sponsorship plan to encourage the use of renewable energy sources and energy savings in residences 2021
type_of_funding: National funds by RES and Energy Conservation Fund

intervention_type: Regulation
project_body:
In April 2018, the Minister of Energy and Resources commissioned an independent review of New Zealand’s electricity market.

The final report was delivered to the Minister of Energy and Resources in May 2019. It contains 32 recommendations related to consumers, industry, and regulation and technology. The Government announced its response to the Review in October 2019 and the Authority welcomes the Government’s response. The review took into account crossovers and linkages with existing and planned work, including: the Electricity Authority's work on transmission pricing the new Interim Climate Change Committee’s work on how to reach 100% renewable electricity by 2035 the Productivity Commission's low emissions economy inquiry. The Authority is taking deliberate and planned action on the Electricity Price Review’s recommendations.

The dashboard of the Electricity Price Review:

- Reducing energy hardship: Establish a cross-sector energy hardship group
- Define energy hardship
- Build network of community level support services
- Prohibit prompt payment discounts
- Strengthening the consumer voice: Establish the consumer advocacy council Improving the regulatory system
- Amend the Electricity Industry Act
- Phase out low fixed-charge tariff regulations
- Update the Electricity Authority’s compliance framework
- Preparing for a low-carbon future
- Explore new institutional arrangements for energy policy and regulation Improving transmission and distribution Issue a GPS on transmission and distribution pricing
- New powers for the Commerce Commission to regulate distributors.
type_of_funding: National funds

intervention_type: Policy Support
project_body:
The Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment (MBIE) had established the panel following a recommendation to government by the independent Electricity Price Review. This panel had a key role in advancing the government’s initiatives to alleviate energy hardship, and the five members appointed for their specialist knowledge and expertise across a range of areas. The members also had experience working directly with households in or at risk of energy hardship, and collectively have insight into the lived experience of energy hardship, particularly of Maori and Pasifika, disabled people, low-income families and other groups at higher risk. The panel members were also leaders in their field or community, and as a group have insight into the broader societal issues that contribute to energy hardship.

The panel provided impartial, evidence-based expert advice and identify where existing energy hardship initiatives can be improved or better leveraged. The panel’s work programme will in part be drawn from the Electricity Price Review’s energy hardship findings and the government’s response to its recommendations, and will be refined when the Panel convenes. The panel members were appointed until 30 June 2023 and worked alongside the soon-to-be-established Energy Hardship Reference Group that is being set up to provide a forum to share information and encourage coordination across industry, NGOs and government agencies. The panel was consisted of 20 members from academia, community groups, industry and was selected for the group, along with 6 representatives from government agencies and a regulator.
type_of_funding: National

intervention_type: Regulation
project_body: The law allows energy-vulnerable consumers to pay their bills considering their entire living situation. The Energy Efficiency law obliges large utilities to open centers for consumers. It can be considered a good practice due to the target group, the human approach, collaboration with social institutions, employing social workers in a utility.
type_of_funding: National funds by the state owned utility Wien Energie

intervention_type: Characterisation; Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Policy Support; Regulation
project_body:
The objective of BIRTUOSS is to upgrade and expand the Opengela service for integrated home renovation, including a wider target of homeowners, creating collaborations with local stakeholders, defining a governance structure with a sustainable business model, and extending the service to the whole Basque Region. Aiming for renovation of multi-family buildings and energy performance improvements , BIRTUOSS will depart from the HIROSS4all project experience to improve the Opengela service acting on lessons learnt and a co-creation process and expand it to several municipalities across the Basque Region.

BIRTUOSS will implement a pilot (living lab) to test the model and will be extended to 9 additional one-stop shops during the scope of the project, covering 4,800 inhabitants and 2,102 dwellings. This will be reflected in primary energy savings of 62.83% (11,41 GWh/year) and a reduction of greenhouse gas emissions of 13,028.54 tCO2/year.

During the 5 years after the project ends, it aims to achieve 10 more, with a total 6,350 inhabitants and 2,744 dwellings. The BIRTUOSS consortium (led by the Basque Government) will address properly the LIFE-CET-HOMERENO topic requirements and will adapt to the regional scenario in which the project is to be carried out and then replicated at European level. It comprises 14 partners covering the whole renovation value chain. Additional entities have been included with an advisory role to fulfill the needs to achieve the ambitious goals.
project_title: BIRTUOSS (Building the Basque Integrated Renovation model (Opengela)
type_of_funding: LIFE programme

intervention_type: Financial mechanisms
project_body: The “Clean Air” programme is Poland’s national subsidy scheme supporting renovations and heat source replacements in single-family houses . Its aim is to reduce energy consumption and reduce sources of air pollution. The reform consists in introducing a more sophisticated and streamlined subsidy design to better address the needs of beneficiaries from different income groups and broaden participation of homeowners with lower incomes in the programme.
type_of_funding: Public

intervention_type: Policy Support; Regulation; Financial mechanisms
project_body: USAID’s Energy Security Project (ESP) works with Ukrainian government, private sector, and civil society leaders to improve Ukraine’s energy security, and transform Ukraine’s energy sector into a modern, market-oriented, EU-integrated, engine of growth.
type_of_funding: USAID

intervention_type: Transparency and information sharing; Regulation
partners_involved: EnEffect - Centre for Energy Efficiency addres
project_body:
SHEERenov+ is proposed as an extension of the H2020 SHEERenov project. The general project objective is to replicate the model for Seamless Home Energy Efficiency Renovation service provision in Bulgaria. Building on the sustainable results of the H2020 SHEERenov project, the LIFE 2022 SHEERenov+ project aims to trigger a significant upscale of the energy renovation in the Bulgarian residential sector, through developing a network of business model OSSs, positioned on municipal level and offering services through the entire value chain.

Besides the capital Sofia, the project activities will take place in 5 more major cities: Plovdiv, Gabrovo, Stara Zagora, Burgas, and Ruse, ensuring the delivery of the integrated home renovation service throughout the whole country.
project_title: SHEERenovPlus (Seamless Services for Housing Energy Efficiency Renovation)
type_of_funding: LIFE programme

intervention_type: Communication campaign; Consumer Advice, protection and empowerment
project_body:
The MultiHome project will establish self-sufficient integrated home renovation model encompassing strong technical expertise and multi-level cooperation with the renovation market stakeholders to accelerate the implementation of the Renovation Wave and its supporting policies in Plovdiv Region .

Thanks to its structured cooperation with all 18 Plovdiv municipalities as well as building/homeowners and renovation providers in Plovdiv Region, the MultiHome project will develop a dedicated Service hub and Platform that will support 1 700 dwellings in single- and multi-apartment buildings in implementing ambitious, deep renovation actions throughout the project lifetime and beyond. It will also develop a long-term roadmap and policy interception documents to support the future renovation of the residential building stock in the region.

The MultiHome project envisages numerous interactive and participatory live events for networking and promoting advanced renovation solutions to the building/homeowners, incl. Marketplace brokerage and match-making events, Renovation Champions and Trusted Supplier Roadshows, and others. It will also develop Citizen Renovation Toolbox and “Trusted supplier” label ensuring high quality and professional conduct in enhanced renovation value chains. It will cooperate with financial bodies to elaborate financial models to support the building/homeowners in finding match funding for their renovation. Also, the MultiHome project will provide knowledge and capacity transfer for 5 local authorities and 2 other regions as well as numerous EU practitioners.
project_title: MultiHome (Stakeholder Cooperation Service Hub and Platform for Accelerated Home Renovation in Plovdiv Region)
type_of_funding: LIFE programme

countries_impacted: Bulgaria; Croatia; Estonia; Hungary; Latvia; Poland; Romania; Slovakia; Ukraine
intervention_type: Capacity building and training; Financial mechanisms
partners_involved: Bankwatch Network
professionals_involved: Researcher; Member of a local/national authority; Civil society employee
project_body: The team developing and implementing the RePower the Regions project is helping regions in Central and Eastern Europe to accelerate the decarbonisation process. The consortium consists of nine countries located in just transition regions (Bulgaria, Czechia, Estonia, Hungary, Latvia, Poland, Romania, Slovakia and Ukraine) and aims at developing the models for implementing inclusive and regional clean energy plans. Holistic solutions, such as local and community-led renewable energy initiatives are implemented.
project_title: RePower the Regions (RePower the Regions: Ambitious and inclusive clean energy plans for repowering the just transition regions)
type_of_funding: LIFE Programme

countries_impacted: Italy; France; Spain; Latvia; Hungary; Ireland
intervention_type: Communication campaign; Monitoring and Impact Assessment; Capacity building and training
partners_involved: Climate Alliance; GBCe - GBC España; ENEA; Ekubirojs (ESEB); Regione Piemonte; Green Building Council España
project_body: The project aims to establish an EU-specific framework to overcome barriers to residential energy renovation. They plan to create a European Community of Practice of Integrated Home Renovation Services (IHRS) with around 600 members. Seven community platforms will be established across six Member States to enhance the IHRS model implementation. Other activities include capacity building, policy dialogues and consulting 150 public authorities.
project_title: EU Peers (European Practitioners for Integrated Home Renovation Services)
type_of_funding: LIFE programme

countries_impacted: Austria; Bulgaria; Germany; Ireland; Slovenia; Spain
intervention_type: Data collection; Policy Support
project_body:
The project recognises that individual energy production may not be feasible or efficient for all citizens. Instead, energy communities offer a collective approach to generating, managing, and distributing renewable energy within specific communities. By pooling resources and expertise, these communities can optimise energy production and distribution, ensuring that energy is accessible and sustainable for all residents.

The POWER-E-Com project addresses the varying regulatory frameworks for energy communities across EU countries. By developing models tailored to each partner country’s specific requirements, the project seeks to provide a comprehensive and adaptable framework for establishing and operating energy communities. This will enable stakeholders to navigate the complexities of legal and regulatory aspects, fostering a more consistent and supportive environment for community energy initiatives.

Measures

- Developing models for the establishment of energy communities : The project will develop models that can be adapted to the specific needs of each participating country. These models will provide guidance on the legal, financial, and organizational aspects of setting up an energy community.
- Providing training and capacity building : The project will provide training and capacity building for citizens and officials on community energy topics. This will help to raise awareness and build the skills necessary to participate in community energy projects.
- Facilitating the exchange of knowledge and experience: The project will facilitate the exchange of knowledge and experience between partner countries. This will help to share best practices and develop new approaches to community energy development.
project_title: POWER-E-COM (Empowering Renewable and Citizen Energy Communities)
type_of_funding: LIFE programme

countries_impacted: Austria; Germany; Italy; Lithuania; Poland; Ukraine
intervention_type: Capacity building and training; Policy Support; Data collection
project_body:
The overall objective of the SUPPORT DHC project is to support a fast implementation of low-grade renewable energy (RE) and waste heat (WH) for district heating and cooling (DHC) in Europe . This is achieved by supporting DHC operators in drawing-up transformation plans leading to efficient DHC systems as defined in the Energy Efficiency Directive revision and in particular concrete investment plans for a fast implementation of packages of low-grade RE and WH measures. In six European countries, the SUPPORT DHC project in cooperation with involved DHC operators demonstrates such processes and leads to investments on the ground for a variety of EU-wide DHC system cases.

The support of DHC operators is rolled-out through a 3-step approach:

- Extensive support is provided to 11 DHC frontrunner cases,
- Minimum 27 follower cases are selected during the project and supported
- More than 100 DHC operators are reached by replicating support services also in countries beyond the SUPPORT DHC target countries.

The project further aims at capacity building of DHC operators for managing and steering such processes and of the specific service providers and other stakeholders for supporting transformation and investment planning. At policy and regulatory level, local and regional authorities will become enablers of transformation measures as the project addresses solutions for removing barriers and framework shortcomings.

Furthermore, also at national and EU levels, market and framework barriers and solutions will be identified and fed into the political decision-making processes. Know-how gained in the SUPPORT DHC project will be disseminated to further EU DHC markets and replication will be actively organised.
project_title: SUPPORT-DHC (Supporting a fast implementation of low-grade renewable energy and waste heat for district heating and cooling)
type_of_funding: LIFE programme

intervention_type: Household energy efficiency and refurbishment; Household energy efficiency and refurbishment
project_body:
The primary goal of the LIFE heritageHOME project, known as "Integrated Renovation Services for Heritage Homes of Estonia” is to pioneer a comprehensive renovation service that guides owners of historic homes in Estonia through the process of enhancing their properties' energy efficiency.

LIFE heritageHOME builds upon the principles and guidance laid out in the 'European Heritage Green Paper’ and engages a range of national policy makers, heritage operators, researchers and representatives of civil society to facilitate the uptake of these principles in Estonian context. The solutions developed and tested will set up a model on how to facilitate energy-efficient renovations within historical building contexts and equip it with necessary toolbox. Moreover, the project places a strong emphasis on involving homeowners in co-creating solutions and collaborating with scientific and professional communities to harness best practices tailored for heritage homes.

The ultimate aim is to assemble an integrated set of services that effectively surmounts the obstacles impeding energy-efficient renovations for heritage properties.
